summary: Coming from Canada, I noticed a lot of misconceptions Americans have about my home country. I grew up in Toronto with my mom, dad, and brother, and we have a very close relationship despite the distance. Weapons and things like the Confederate flag are not common in Canada, so moving to the South in the United States was a big cultural shock for me. I often reflect on how different our cultures are, even though we're geographically close.

I've been living in the United States for nearly six years now, mostly in the South, which was a big adjustment for me. The presence of guns and the Confederate flag everywhere was something I had never experienced back in Canada. I plan to stay here as long as my visa allows, hoping to find a teaching job and build on the relationships I've made. The stronger American dollar also makes working here a practical choice for me.

Before moving to the United States, I had only seen the Confederate flag in textbooks, but living in the South, I saw it everywhere, which was quite surprising. It stood out as a symbol that I wasn’t used to and made me realize how different the cultural landscape is here compared to Canada. The flag’s presence was one of the first things I noticed and it made me think about how Americans view their history differently than I do.

Toronto is where my family still lives—my mom, dad, and brother—and we remain very close despite the distance. It’s hard being so far apart, especially with the added challenge of expensive shipping across the border. I’m eagerly looking forward to them coming to my graduate school graduation in May 2019, which will be a special moment for all of us.
